notes.note: Third consecutive recommendation letter to M'. Acilius Glabrio, proconsul of Sicily; this one on behalf of the Clodius brothers of Halaesa. The same proconsular address formula (ACILIO PROCONSVLI) confirms the same recipient as 13.30--31.

notes.anchor: In the town of Halaesa, a place of such elegance and such standing
notes.note: Halaesa (modern Tusa, northern Sicily) was among the most prosperous and Hellenised of the inland Sicilian towns, famous for its civic order and wealthy elite; Cicero celebrated it in the Verrine orations as a model community. Lauta (elegant/wealthy) and nobilis (distinguished) are the precise compliments.

notes.anchor: both by ties of hospitality and by intimate friendship, the brothers Marcus and Gaius Clodius --- Archagathus and Philo
notes.note: The two brothers bear a Roman nomen (Clodius) alongside Greek cognomina (Archagathus, Philo), the typical pattern for Sicilian elite families enfranchised under the late Republic; their double connection to Cicero --- hospitium (formal guest-friendship) and familiaritas (intimate personal friendship) --- places them at the top tier of his Sicilian network.

notes.anchor: I may seem to be levelling out my recommendations by some sort of canvasser's habit
notes.note: Cicero acknowledges the rhetorical risk of over-recommending: a letter of introduction loses force if the recipient suspects it is merely formulaic (ambitio here means something like inflated courtesy rather than electoral canvassing). His self-correction is a standard captatio benevolentiae device in the Book 13 recommendation series.
